ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for seaweed salad as a prepared dish; closest_parent 'seaweed food product' (FOODON_03311434) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers seaweeds and other algae as a category; no specific prepared seaweed salad code exists in FoodEx2. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 44027008 refers to seaweed as a substance; no specific prepared seaweed salad concept exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of seaweed salad is approximately 100 g, presented as a small side dish or appetiser.

nutrient_source.title: Seaweed salad, Japanese-style (Chuka wakame) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for wakame (raw, NDB 11667) and seasoned seaweed salad (Chuka wakame) composite data. Values reflect a typical Japanese-style seaweed salad with sesame oil, soy sauce, rice vinegar, and sesame seeds dressing. Energy and macros cross-referenced against FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for seaweed-based dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Japanese-style seaweed salad. GI estimated at 16 based on wakame seaweed as the primary ingredient; wakame and mixed seaweed are classified as very low GI foods (GI ~15–17) in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021). The sesame oil and vinegar dressing further suppresses glycaemic response. GL per 100 g serving = (16 × 6.5) / 100 = 1.04, rounded to 1.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 100 g serving of seaweed salad provides approximately 520 mg sodium, which represents roughly 22% of the recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg; portion control and requesting reduced-sodium dressing are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Seaweed salad has a very low glycemic index (estimated GI ~16) and provides only 6.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 1, making it a suitable side dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable fatty acid profile with omega-3 content and zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Seaweed salad contains no cholesterol, a low saturated fat content (0.6 g per 100 g), and beneficial polyunsaturated fatty acids from sesame oil and seaweed, supporting a heart-healthy dietary pattern; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention in individuals with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seaweed salad (海藻サラダ, kaiso sarada) is a Japanese-style cold appetiser or side dish made primarily from rehydrated wakame seaweed and sometimes a blend of other edible seaweeds such as agar strands and hijiki, dressed with a seasoned marinade of sesame oil, rice vinegar, soy sauce, sugar, and chilli flakes, and garnished with toasted sesame seeds. The dish has a tender, slightly slippery texture with a mild oceanic flavour balanced by the nutty, tangy, and lightly sweet dressing. It is served chilled and is a common accompaniment to sushi, sashimi, and bento meals in Japanese restaurants worldwide, including in Singapore where it appears on menus at conveyor-belt sushi chains such as Sushi Express. Nutritionally, seaweed salad is low in calories (approximately 70 kcal per 100 g), low in carbohydrates (6.5 g), and provides a modest amount of plant protein (1.7 g) and dietary fibre (1.5 g). It is a source of iodine, calcium, magnesium, and iron from the seaweed base. The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI ~16), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose levels. The primary clinical consideration is sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce-based seasoning, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally vegan, dairy-free, and gluten-free when tamari is used in place of regular soy sauce, though standard preparations contain soy and sesame allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in seaweed salad?
Is seaweed salad good for diabetics?
Is seaweed salad high in sodium?
What seaweed is used in Japanese seaweed salad?
Can I eat seaweed salad if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Japanese-style seaweed salad exists in a single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central wakame and seasoned seaweed salad composite data, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data. GI is an analogous estimate based on wakame seaweed GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides a direct exact match for this prepared dish.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source database for this food item; seaweed can contain variable vitamin D2 depending on UV exposure but this is not reliably quantified in standard food composition tables. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero as the dish contains no hydrogenated oils. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is reported as 0.0; seaweed salad made from wakame contains negligible or no bioavailable B12 per USDA FoodData Central data — confirmed zero for this preparation. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero as all ingredients are plant-derived. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~16) based on wakame seaweed classification in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021); no direct GI measurement for prepared seaweed salad was located. GL per 100 g serving = (16 × 6.5) / 100 ≈ 1.0. 6. Sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) is the primary clinical concern and reflects typical restaurant-style seasoning with soy sauce; home preparations may vary. 7. Allergens include soy (from soy sauce) and sesame (from sesame oil and seeds); gluten may be present if regular soy sauce is used. 8. The food is classified as Japanese cuisine; mandatory multilingual field is Japanese (satisfied). Chinese simplified and traditional names are provided as supplementary context given Singapore restaurant context.
